steps.title: What is 3D Modelling?
steps.content:
What is 3D Modelling?

3D modelling is the process of creating a digital three-dimensional object on a computer. Real engineers, architects, animators and product designers use 3D software every day to design and test objects before they are built in the real world.

Where is 3D modelling used?

  • Engineering & manufacturing — designing car parts, bridges, machines
  • Architecture — visualising buildings before construction
  • Product design — creating phone cases, furniture, packaging
  • 3D printing — turning digital models into real physical objects
  • Games & film — characters, environments and special effects
  • Medicine — modelling bones, organs and custom prosthetics

CAD stands for Computer-Aided Design. CAD software lets you draw precise 3D shapes using exact measurements — very different from freehand artistic tools. In this course you will use Chili3D, a free, browser-based CAD tool.

Check your understanding:

steps.title: The Chili3D Interface
steps.content:
The Chili3D Interface

Chili3D is a browser-based CAD application. Before you start building, learn the key areas of the screen.

Key areas to find when you open Chili3D:

  • ① Toolbar (top) — New file, save, undo/redo, and view controls
  • ② Command toolbar (left) — Buttons for creating shapes (Box, Cylinder, Sphere, etc.) and modifying them
  • ③ 3D Viewport (centre) — The main working area where your model appears
  • ④ Model tree (left panel) — A list of every object in your scene
  • ⑤ Properties panel (right) — Appears when an object is selected; shows dimensions and settings

Navigating the 3D view

  • Orbit (rotate) — Hold middle mouse button and drag, or right-click drag
  • Zoom — Scroll wheel
  • Pan — Hold Shift + middle mouse button and drag

Open the editor below and spend 2 minutes exploring — find all five areas listed above before moving on.

steps.title: Your First Shape: A Box
steps.content:
Your First Shape: A Box

The Box is the most common starting shape in CAD. Follow these steps carefully — every CAD tool uses a similar process.

Step-by-step: Create a Box

  1. Look at the left-side toolbar for a Box button (a cube icon) and click it
  2. Move your mouse into the 3D viewport — you will see a preview box following your cursor
  3. Click once to place the first corner of the box
  4. Move the mouse to set the width and depth, then click again
  5. Move the mouse up or down to set the height, then click a third time to finish
  6. Your box now appears in the viewport and in the model tree on the left
  7. Click the box to select it — you should see its dimensions in the properties panel on the right

💡 Tip — Undo

Made a mistake? Press Ctrl+Z to undo, just like any other application.

steps.title: More Basic Shapes
steps.content:
More Basic Shapes

All 3D CAD models start from primitive shapes — simple forms that are combined and modified to create complex objects. The three most important primitives are the Box, the Cylinder, and the Sphere.

Shape Looks like Real-world examples
BoxCube or cuboidBuilding, phone, brick, room
CylinderRound tube with flat endsTin can, pillar, pipe, wheel
SpherePerfectly round ballBall, planet, bubble, dome

Your task:

  1. Start a new file (File → New, or the New button in the toolbar)
  2. Create one Cylinder using the Cylinder tool in the left toolbar
  3. Create one Sphere next to it
  4. Click each shape to select it and check its name appears in the model tree

steps.title: Check Your Knowledge
steps.content:
Check Your Knowledge

Answer these questions about what you have learnt so far.

steps.title: Practice: Build a Simple Scene
steps.content:
Practice: Build a Simple Scene

Use what you have learnt to build a simple scene in Chili3D. This is your chance to experiment — there is no single correct answer.

🎯 Challenge — Build a table

Using only Box shapes, build a simple table:

  • One wide flat box for the table top
  • Four tall thin boxes for the legs — one at each corner

Hint: create the table top first, then create each leg separately and position them underneath. You can move objects by selecting them and dragging.

Extension — if you finish early

  • Add a Sphere on top of the table as a decorative bowl or vase
  • Add a Cylinder as a cup or glass
  • Experiment with the view — try looking at your scene from above, from the side, and from the front
